section.title: The Conversion of Saul
section.sectionRationale: The great persecutor becomes the great apostle. Saul, still breathing threats and armed with letters to arrest believers in Damascus, is stopped on the road by the risen Christ, who identifies Himself with the church Saul is hunting. Blinded and humbled, he is sent to a fearful Ananias, filled with the Spirit, baptized, and at once begins to preach that Jesus is the Son of God. This is the calling of the man who will carry the name of Christ to the Gentiles.
section.thesis: The risen Jesus sovereignly and graciously arrests His fiercest enemy, revealing that to persecute the church is to persecute Christ Himself, and turns Saul into a chosen instrument to carry His name to the nations, so that no one is beyond the reach of grace and no life is too far gone to be remade for mission.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Saul breathing threats on the way to Damascus; the light, the voice, and three days blind; Ananias sent to the chosen instrument; Saul preaching Christ boldly in Damascus and escaping the plot; and his wary reception in Jerusalem as the church finds peace.

units.label: Saul breathing threats
units.note: the persecutor sets out for Damascus

verses.text: Meanwhile, Saul was still breathing out murderous threats against the disciples of the Lord. He approached the high priest
and requested letters to the synagogues in Damascus, so that if he found any men or women belonging to the {{hodos|Way}}, he could bring them as prisoners to Jerusalem.

units.label: The light on the road
units.note: Saul, why do you persecute Me

verses.text: As Saul drew near to Damascus on his journey, suddenly a light from heaven flashed around him.
He fell to the ground and heard a voice say to him, “Saul, Saul, why do you {{dioko|persecute}} Me?”
“Who are You, Lord?” Saul asked. “I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting,” He replied.
“Now get up and go into the city, and you will be told what you must do.”
The men traveling with Saul stood there speechless. They heard the voice but did not see anyone.
Saul got up from the ground, but when he opened his eyes he could not see a thing. So they led him by the hand into Damascus.
For three days he was without sight, and he did not eat or drink anything.

units.label: Ananias and the chosen instrument
units.note: the Lord sends a fearful disciple to Saul

verses.text: In Damascus there was a disciple named Ananias. The Lord spoke to him in a vision, “Ananias!” “Here I am, Lord,” he answered.
“Get up!” the Lord told him. “Go to the house of Judas on Straight Street and ask for a man from Tarsus named Saul, for he is praying.
In a vision he has seen a man named Ananias come and place his hands on him to restore his sight.”
But Ananias answered, “Lord, many people have told me about this man and all the harm he has done to Your saints in Jerusalem.
And now he is here with authority from the chief priests to arrest all who call on Your name.”
“Go!” said the Lord. “This man is My chosen {{skeuos|instrument}} to carry My name before the Gentiles and their kings, and before the people of Israel.
I will show him how much he must suffer for My name.”

So Ananias went to the house, and when he arrived, he placed his hands on Saul. “Brother Saul,” he said, “the Lord Jesus, who appeared to you on the road as you were coming here, has sent me so that you may see again and be filled with the Holy Spirit.”
At that instant, something like scales fell from Saul’s eyes, and his sight was restored. He got up and was baptized,
and after taking some food, he regained his strength. And he spent several days with the disciples in Damascus.

units.label: Saul preaches at Damascus
units.note: proving that Jesus is the Christ

verses.text: Saul promptly began to proclaim Jesus in the synagogues, declaring, “He is the Son of God.”
All who heard him were astounded and asked, “Isn’t this the man who wreaked havoc in Jerusalem on those who call on this name? And hasn’t he come here to take them as prisoners to the chief priests?”
But Saul was empowered all the more, and he confounded the Jews living in Damascus by proving that Jesus is the {{christos|Christ}}.
After many days had passed, the Jews conspired to kill him,
but Saul learned of their plot. Day and night they watched the city gates in order to kill him.
One night, however, his disciples took him and lowered him in a basket through a window in the wall.

units.label: Saul in Jerusalem and the church at peace
units.note: Barnabas vouches for him, and the church is strengthened

verses.text: When Saul arrived in Jerusalem, he tried to join the disciples, but they were all afraid of him, not believing that he was a disciple.
Then Barnabas brought him to the apostles and described how Saul had seen the Lord, who had spoken to him on the road to Damascus, and how Saul had spoken boldly in that city in the name of Jesus.
So Saul stayed with them, moving about freely in Jerusalem and speaking boldly in the name of the Lord.
He talked and debated with the Grecian Jews, but they tried to kill him.
When the brothers learned of this, they took him down to Caesarea and sent him off to Tarsus.
Then the church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria experienced a time of peace. It grew in strength and numbers, living in the fear of the Lord and the encouragement of the Holy Spirit.

hodos.gloss: A road or way. In Acts the Way becomes an early name for the Christian movement, those who follow Jesus who called Himself the way. It is fitting that Saul, hunting followers of the Way, is stopped on a literal road and set on a new path.

dioko.gloss: To pursue, to persecute. The risen Christ asks why Saul persecutes Me, not merely My people, revealing that the Lord is so united to His church that to attack them is to attack Him. This union grounds Paul's later theology of the body of Christ.

skeuos.gloss: A vessel or instrument. Christ calls Saul a chosen vessel to carry His name to the nations. The word underlines grace: Saul is not chosen for his worthiness but taken up and put to use by the Lord who saved him.

christos.gloss: The Anointed One, the Messiah. Saul's whole mission is summed up in proving that Jesus is the Christ, the promised King of Israel, crucified and risen, the same claim that had made him a persecutor now becomes the message he preaches.

basic.context: Saul has been introduced as a violent enemy of the church, and here he is still breathing threats, heading to Damascus with legal papers to arrest believers. On the road the risen Jesus appears in blazing light and asks why Saul is persecuting Him, identifying Himself with His people. Blinded, Saul is led into the city, and God sends a frightened disciple named Ananias to heal and baptize him. At once Saul starts preaching that Jesus is the Son of God. The key questions are: what does it mean that Christ says you are persecuting Me, why is Saul chosen, and how does the church come to trust him?
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as a true conversion and a true commissioning at once. The turning point is not Saul's search for God but God's seizing of Saul, so the chapter magnifies sovereign grace. Christ's words in verse 4 are the theological heart: the union of Christ and His church. The rest describes how a real conversion works out in baptism, bold witness, suffering, and slow acceptance by the wider church.
basic.originalLanguages: Saul is hunting those who belong to the Way, the hodos, when the Lord asks why he persecutes, dioko, Him. Jesus names Saul a chosen instrument, skeuos, a vessel taken up for His purposes. And Saul's message becomes the proof that Jesus is the Christos, the Messiah. The words move from persecution to mission in a few strokes.
basic.typology: The scales falling from Saul's eyes picture the spiritual blindness that only Christ can lift. His three days without sight, food, or drink, followed by new life, echo a death and resurrection pattern. Ananias, an unremarkable disciple used for a great purpose, shows how God works through ordinary servants.
basic.christ: The whole account turns on the risen Christ. He appears alive, He speaks, He claims to be one with His suffering people, and He commissions Saul. From that moment Saul preaches Christ as the Son of God and proves He is the Messiah. The conversion is not to a philosophy but to a living Lord who confronts, forgives, and sends.
basic.god: God is sovereign and gracious. He stops His enemy in his tracks, chooses him for service before Saul has done a thing to earn it, overrules the fears of Ananias, and protects Saul from plots. He also warns that Saul must suffer for the name, showing that grace and hardship travel together in God's service.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 1 gives Paul's own account of this call by grace. 1 Timothy 1 calls him the foremost of sinners shown mercy. 1 Corinthians 15 lists the risen Christ appearing to him last of all. Acts 26 retells the road to Damascus before Agrippa.
basic.oneStory: The chief enemy of the gospel becomes its chief herald. In one conversion God shows that no rebel is beyond His reach, and He raises up the apostle who will carry the name of Jesus across the empire to the Gentiles, driving the story of Acts toward the ends of the earth.

context.literary: This is the third telling of Saul's importance in Acts and the first full narrative of his conversion, which Luke will repeat twice more in chapters 22 and 26, marking its weight. It sits between the Samaritan and eunuch mission of chapter 8 and the Gentile breakthrough of chapters 10 and 11, preparing the reader for the apostle to the nations.
context.historical: Damascus lay outside Judea, and the high priest's letters reflect a reach into diaspora synagogues to extradite believers. The Way was a Jewish renewal movement centred on Jesus, still worshipping in synagogues, which is why Saul both hunts it there and later preaches in the same buildings. Escape in a basket through the wall matches Paul's own memory in 2 Corinthians 11.
context.canonical: Saul's calling is a hinge of the New Testament, since thirteen letters bear his name and the second half of Acts follows his missions. His conversion becomes the pattern of grace to the undeserving and the ground of his gospel, that righteousness is a gift and not a wage, learned the day the risen Christ met His enemy.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke narrates the sovereign conversion and commissioning of the church's fiercest opponent to show that the risen Christ is alive and gathering the nations, to ground Paul's apostolic authority, and to display grace strong enough to save and to send even a persecutor.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The blinding light and audible voice describe a unique, foundational calling of an apostle, not a template every conversion must copy. What is prescriptive is the reality of grace seizing sinners, the union of Christ with His people, the call to bold witness, and the truth that following Christ includes suffering for His name.

debates.question: Was Saul's Damascus road experience a conversion, a prophetic commissioning, or both?

views.label: A genuine conversion and a commissioning together
views.case: The strongest case holds both at once: Saul is truly converted, turned from enmity to faith, baptized, filled with the Spirit, and forgiven, and in the same act called as an apostle to the Gentiles. Luke's language of light, blindness, and new sight is conversion imagery, while the words about carrying the name are commissioning; the letters confirm Paul saw himself as a sinner saved and an apostle sent.

views.label: Chiefly a prophetic call of an already devout man
views.case: Some stress the parallels with prophetic call scenes and cast it mainly as the commissioning of a zealous but misdirected worshipper of the true God, downplaying the word conversion. This rightly notes the call motifs, but it underplays Paul's own testimony that he was a blasphemer and persecutor shown mercy, and that he counted his former standing as loss for Christ.

debates.bearing: Holding conversion and commission together protects both the depth of grace, that even a persecutor is forgiven and made new, and the authority of the apostle who was sent by the risen Lord Himself.

study.originalLanguages: Saul is arresting those who belong to the hodos, the Way, when the risen Lord asks why he persecutes, dioko, Him, not merely His followers, exposing the union of Christ with His church. Jesus names him a chosen skeuos, a vessel or instrument, taken up by grace for a purpose Saul never sought. And the persecutor becomes the preacher who proves that Jesus is the Christos, the Messiah of Israel, crucified and risen.

typology.type: The scales falling from Saul's eyes
typology.fulfillment: A picture of spiritual blindness lifted only by Christ, as the persecutor is given sight to see and confess the Lord he opposed.

typology.type: Three days without sight, food, or drink, then new life
typology.fulfillment: A death and resurrection pattern, Saul brought low and then raised to walk in newness of life through Christ.

typology.type: Ananias, an ordinary disciple sent on a hard errand
typology.fulfillment: God's habit of using the unknown and the fearful to accomplish His great purposes, fulfilled again through humble servants of Christ.

study.christ: The risen Jesus dominates the chapter. He appears in glory, speaks, and claims such union with His people that to persecute them is to persecute Him. He forgives His enemy, fills him with the Spirit, and sends him to carry His name. From that hour Saul proclaims Jesus as the Son of God and proves He is the Christ. The conversion is Christ's own work, and the message is Christ Himself, the crucified and living Lord.
study.god: God's sovereign grace is on full display. He interrupts a hardened enemy, chooses him before he has earned anything, and answers the fear of Ananias with a plain command to go. He guards Saul through murderous plots and grants the whole church a season of peace and growth. Yet He also appoints suffering for the sake of the name, weaving cost into calling. Salvation and mission alike are God's initiative.

crossReferences.note: Paul's own account that his gospel and apostleship came by revelation of Jesus Christ, not from man.
Paul the former blasphemer and persecutor held up as the pattern of Christ's perfect patience toward sinners.
The risen Christ appeared to Paul last of all, and by grace he became what he was.
Paul recalls his escape from Damascus, lowered in a basket through the wall, confirming this narrative.

study.oneStory: In one road-side encounter the fiercest foe of the church is remade into its greatest missionary. The risen Christ proves He is alive and one with His people, and He commissions the man who will carry His name to the Gentiles and their kings. The whole sweep of Acts toward the ends of the earth now has its chosen instrument, a living monument to grace that saves the very worst.

sermon.title: One Changed Life (Acts 9:1-18)
sermon.why: Begg preaches Saul's conversion as the work of God's sovereign, gradual, gentle grace, showing that the same grace that seized the church's fiercest enemy is what frees and remakes any sinner, which is precisely the heart of this study.

quiz.q: What did the risen Jesus reveal by asking, why do you persecute Me?

options.text: That He is so united to His church that attacking His people is attacking Him.
options.note: Christ identifies Himself with the disciples Saul is hunting, the union of head and body.

options.text: That Saul had personally harmed Jesus during His earthly ministry.
options.note: Saul had persecuted the church, not the earthly Jesus; the point is Christ's union with His people now.

options.text: That Saul's opposition mattered to God because the disciples were suffering.
options.note: True that their suffering mattered, but the striking claim is that Christ counts it as done to Himself.

options.text: That Jesus was merely a memory the disciples clung to.
options.note: The living Christ speaks in person, disproving any idea that He was only a memory.

quiz.q: Why did the Lord choose Saul, according to the passage?

options.text: As a chosen instrument to carry Christ's name to the Gentiles, their kings, and Israel.
options.note: The Lord states this purpose to Ananias in verse 15.

options.text: Because Saul was a learned and zealous man whose gifts God could use.
options.note: His gifts were later used, but the text grounds the choice in God's purpose and grace, not Saul's worthiness.

options.text: To make him a witness who would also suffer much for the name.
options.note: True and stated in verse 16, but this is the cost attached to the mission, not the mission's main aim.

options.text: Because Saul had begun to soften and seek Christ on his own.
options.note: Saul was breathing threats, not seeking Christ; the initiative was entirely the Lord's.

quiz.q: How is the Damascus road event best understood?

options.text: As a genuine conversion and an apostolic commissioning at the same time.
options.note: Saul is truly saved and truly sent, both together, as Luke and Paul's letters show.

options.text: As mainly the prophetic call of an already devout worshipper of God.
options.note: This underplays Paul's own testimony that he was a persecutor shown mercy and made new.

options.text: As a conversion whose missionary calling unfolded over the years that followed.
options.note: Partly right, since the mission did unfold over time, but the commission was given at the outset, not only later.

options.text: As a vision with no lasting effect on Saul's life or beliefs.
options.note: The effect was total and permanent; the persecutor became the preacher at once.

quiz.q: Why were the Jerusalem disciples slow to accept Saul?

options.text: They were afraid of him and did not believe he was really a disciple.
options.note: Verse 26 states their fear and disbelief, until Barnabas vouched for him.

options.text: They were unsure whether his preaching in Damascus had been genuine.
options.note: Reasonable, and Barnabas addressed it by testifying to his bold witness, but the text names their fear that he was not a disciple at all.

options.text: They thought he needed more training before joining them.
options.note: The issue was trust and fear, not a lack of training.

options.text: They had never heard of Saul or his reputation.
options.note: They knew his reputation well, which is exactly why they were afraid.

quiz.q: What does Ananias's role teach about how God works?

options.text: God uses ordinary, even fearful, disciples to accomplish His great purposes.
options.note: Ananias is an unknown believer sent on a daunting errand, and God works powerfully through him.

options.text: God only entrusts important tasks to well-known leaders.
options.note: The opposite is shown; a little-known disciple is the one sent to the future apostle.

options.text: God expects His servants to obey even when they are afraid or reluctant.
options.note: True and important, but the fuller lesson is that God delights to use ordinary servants, not only that they must obey.

options.text: God rewards Ananias for volunteering eagerly for the task.
options.note: Ananias did not volunteer eagerly; he objected first, then obeyed the Lord's command.

questions: Christ told Saul that persecuting the church was persecuting Him. How does knowing that Jesus takes His people's suffering personally shape the way you treat other believers?
Saul was chosen while he was still an enemy, before he had done anything to deserve it. Where do you need to hear that grace comes to you as a gift and not a wage?
Ananias was afraid, yet he obeyed and went to Saul. Is there a hard step of obedience you have been avoiding because of fear of a particular person?
The Lord said Saul must suffer for the name. How does it steady you to know that following Christ can include real cost, not only comfort?
Barnabas took a risk to welcome a former enemy into the church. Who might God be calling you to receive or believe the best about, when others are still wary?

translationNotes.note: The BSB ends Jesus' reply at, I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. A footnote records that the later Textus Receptus adds, It is hard for you to kick against the goads, words that appear in Paul's own account in Acts 26:14; the BSB keeps them where the manuscripts best attest them.

The men heard the voice but saw no one. Paul's later retelling in Acts 22:9 says his companions did not understand the voice; the two accounts are reconciled by noting that they heard a sound without grasping its meaning.
The Grecian Jews, or Hellenists, were Greek-speaking Jews, the same group from which Stephen's opponents had come; Saul now debates the very circle he once served.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 9:1-31. Footnote markers and line-break objects were skipped when building verse text. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon verified this session: Alistair Begg, One Changed Life, Acts 9:1-18, on truthforlife.org.
